<<<KOT:saltshaker>>>>
{"kind":"molecule","gloss":"a thing made of one kind; people put it above food; inside it there is {urn:molecule-v0:ground|ground} [m] salt; when someone moves it in a way, small parts of the salt come out of it.","groundingNote":"a thing made of one kind; people put it above food; inside it there is {urn:molecule-v0:ground|ground} [m] salt; when someone moves it in a way, small parts of the salt come out of it.","groundingRefs":["urn:molecule-v0:ground"]}

<<<KOT:schmear>>>>
{"kind":"molecule","gloss":"something of one kind people put on food; someone can move it with one part of the body onto the food in a way; after someone does this, the food and this something are together in one way.","groundingNote":"something of one kind people put on food; someone can move it with one part of the body onto the food in a way; after someone does this, the food and this something are together in one way.","groundingRefs":[]}
<<<END:schmear>>>>

<<<KOT:seminiferous>>>>
{"kind":"cannot-formalise","why":"This adjective refers to a biological property of tissues (seminiferous tubules) defined by their role in meiosis and spermatozoa production. The underlying mechanism — germ-cell division, gamete formation, and male reproductive physiology — is technical-biological and cannot be grounded in the available prime lexicon (which contains no terms for cells, meiosis, reproduction, or sex-specific anatomy). It belongs to the biology sector, not this lexical tier."}
<<<END:seminiferous>>>>

<<<KOT:shelflike>>>>
{"kind":"cannot-formalise","why":"This is a comparative-form adjective ('resembling a shelf') that expresses a similarity relation to a specific artefact type. While 'shelf' itself might be a molecule, the modifying predicate — resemblance or characteristic structural analogy — requires a complex relational concept (LIKE or BE-SPEC with comparative structure) that the prime lexicon is not designed to support at this level. The concept is fundamentally definitional by reference to another object-type, not by independent sensory or functional properties in the prime vocabulary."}
<<<END:shelflike>>>>
